content:
Consider the sets I, II and III. Identify the set(s) which is (are) correctly matched.

I. Staggered ethane > eclipsed ethane ..........torsional strain.

II. 2, 2-dimethylbutane > 2-methylpentane ......... boiling point

III. cis-but-2-ene > trans-but-2-ene ......... dipole moment
explanation:
Among the given sets, only III is correct i.e.

cis-but-2-ene > trans-but-2-ene (dipole moment) while I and II are incorrect. Their correct form is (I) torsional strain is more in eclipsed ethane than in staggered ethane. It is because in eclipsed form hydrogen atoms attached to two carbons are as closed together as possible while in staggered form hydrogen are as far apart as possible. 2-dimethyl butane < 2-methyl pentane (B.P)

content: Which one of the following compounds does not give benzoic acid when treated with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ ?
explanation: $t$-butyl benzene does not give benzoic acid when treated with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ because it lack the necessary benzylic hydrogen required for oxidation to form benzoic acid, since the tertiary carbon in $t$-butylbenzene has no hydrogen attached directly to it, the oxidation cannot proceeds to form carboxylic acid.

content:
Consider the following

Statement I Kolbe's electrolysis of sodium propionate gives $n$-hexane as product.

Statement II In Kolbe's process $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is liberated at anode and $\mathrm{H}_2$ is liberated at cathode.

Correct answer is

options.content: Both statement-I and statement-II are not correct
Statement-I is correct, but statement-II is not correct
Statement-I is not correct, but statement-II is correct

explanation:
Statement I is wrong, but Statement II is right.

Statement I should say: In Kolbe's electrolysis of sodium propionate, the product is butane, not n-hexane.

The chemical reaction is:

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COONa} + 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3 + 2 \mathrm{CO}_2 + 2 \mathrm{NaOH} + \mathrm{H}_2 \uparrow \\ & \text { Butane } \end{aligned} $$

In Statement II, it is correct that in Kolbe's process, carbon dioxide ($\mathrm{CO}_2$) is released at the anode and hydrogen gas ($\mathrm{H}_2$) is released at the cathode.

content: The catalyst used for the isomerisation of $n$-alkanes to branched chain alkanes is
explanation:
The primary catalyst used for the isomerisation of $n$-alkanes to branch

chain alkanes are acid catalyst, particularly zeolites and sulphated zirconia.

Anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ or HCl can be also used.

content: When ethyl bromide and $n$-propyl bromide are allowed to react with Na metal in dry ether, the number of different alkanes formed is
explanation:
The reaction is a Wurtz reaction, In the given case, three alkanes are formed

$$ 2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[\text { Ether }]{\text { Dry }} \underset{\text { Butane }}{\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}}+2 \mathrm{NaBr} $$

$$ 2 \mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_7 \mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[\text { Ether }]{\text { Dry }} \underset{\text { Hexane }}{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{14}}+2 \mathrm{NaBr} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_7 \mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na} \longrightarrow & \underset{\text { Pentene }}{\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_{12}} +2 \mathrm{NaBr} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \therefore \text { Butane, hexane, pentane are formed. } $$

content:
Which of the following sequence of reagents convert
3- hexene to propane?
explanation:
$\underset{\substack{\text { 3-hexene }}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}}=\mathrm{CHCH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{O}_3}$

$2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{HCl}} \underset{\text { Propane }}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3}$

So, the correct sequence of reagent is (i) $\mathrm{O}_3$ (ii) $\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{HCl}$.

content: In Kolbe's electrolysis of sodium propanoate, products formed at anode and cathode are respectively
explanation:
In Kolbe's electrolysis, the sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid undergoes decarboxylation to form the corresponding alkanes. The overall reaction is outlined below:

$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \, \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COONa} + 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow[\text{electrolysis}]{\text{Kolbe's}} \\ & \underset{\text{(At anode)}}{\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}} + 2 \, \text{CO}_2 + 2 \, \text{NaOH} + \underset{\text{(At cathode)}}{\text{H}_2} \end{aligned} $

Thus, the products at the anode and cathode are:

Anode: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ (butane)

Cathode: $\text{H}_2$ (hydrogen gas)

content: Hydrolysis of an alkyl bromide $\left(\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{Br}\right)$ follows first order kinetics. Reaction of $X$ with Mg in dry ether followed by treatment of $\mathrm{D}_2 \mathrm{O}$ gave $Y$, What is $Y$ ?
A mixture of ethyl iodide and $n$-propyl iodide is subjected to Wurtz reaction. The hydrocarbon which will not be formed is
explanation:
In the Wurtz reaction, alkanes are synthesized by reducing alkyl halides using sodium in dry ether. This process combines two alkyl groups to form higher alkanes.

$ R-X + 2 \mathrm{Na} + X-R_1 \xrightarrow{\text{Dry ether}} R-R_1 + 2 \mathrm{NaI} $

When a mixture of ethyl iodide and $n$-propyl iodide undergoes the reaction, the following higher alkanes are formed:

(i) Butane:

$ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{I} + 2\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{I}-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 \\ &\longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 + 2 \mathrm{NaI} \end{aligned} $

(ii) Hexane:

$ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{I} + 2\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{I}-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 \\ &\longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 + 2 \mathrm{NaI} \end{aligned} $

(iii) Pentane:

$ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{I} + 2\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{I}-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 \\ &\longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{CH}_3 + 2 \mathrm{NaI} \end{aligned} $

Thus, propane is the only hydrocarbon not formed in this reaction.

content: The product of which of the following reactions undergo hydrolysis by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism?
explanation:
$S_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism are generally carried out in polar protic solvents and generally occurs in two steps.

Among the given reactions products of $B$ and $C$ will undergo hydrolysis by $S_N 1$ mechanism, as the products are $2^{\circ} / 3^{\circ}$. benzy bromides which will furnish very stable carbocations.

content: The product formed when a hydrocarbon $$X$$ of molecular formula $$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10}$$ is reacted with sodamide is subjected to ozonolysis, followed by hydrolysis with $$\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$$ and upon further oxidation gave two carboxylic acids, of which one is optically active. The hydrocarbon '$$X$$' is
explanation:
Here for unknown formula given $$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10}$$, we can predict molecular structure of given formula by using double bond equivalent.

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{DBE}=\mathrm{C}-\frac{\mathrm{H}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{N}}{2}-\frac{X}{2}+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad=6-\frac{10}{2}+0-0+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad=6-5+1=2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the hydrocarbon X is 3-methylpent-1-yne.
